texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For  economic  and  industrial  developments,  the  availability  of freshwater is a vital requirement. Water demand is increasing primarily due to population growth, while supply remains a major concern due to the over-extraction of ground and surface water sources. To fulfil the water demand-supply gap, desalination of the sea or brackish water is one of the very few alternatives. Thus, the number of desalination plants and  their  capacity  has  grown  substantially  in  the  last  two  decades (Fig. 1) [1]. Desalination is a dynamic process, which uses more energy as  compared  to  other  water  treatment  options,  and  thus  remains  an expensive  alternative  due  to  rising  energy  prices  and  logistic  supply problems in remote and island communities. To overcome the limitation of energy cost in desalination processes, low-grade heat sources (below 100 ◦ C) can offer an excellent prospect. However, harnessing low-grade heat energy is challenging because of current technology limitations. Extracting  energy  from  sources  with  variable  heat  input  and  small temperature differential from the environment is theoretically difficult as per the second law of thermodynamics [2].

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water and energy are critical components for human, and govern the growth and progress of societies. Desalination is an important process to meet ever-increasing water demand, although it is an energy-intensive alternative  compared  to  conventional  water  treatment  techniques.  Demand  for  desalination  has  been  expanded rapidly as traditional water sources are getting overexploited. Membrane distillation (MD) is a promising water treatment process for highly saline water, including reject from the reverse osmosis plants. To compensate for high energy requirement for the MD process, low-grade heat sources (such as waste heat or renewable sources) can be used. Limitations of other desalination processes are discussed in this review to underline evolution and tweaking of alternatives for making them optimal. In this review, we examine the suitability of the MD process for harnessing energy from different types of low-grade heat sources to make it cost-effective. Financial aspects of the MD process for different heat sources are also highlighted. A summary of studies focusing on hybrid MD systems is also provided. Finally, challenges, opportunities and current implementation of the MD process by using low-grade heat-based MD systems are discussed.

texts.orig: e.,  the  Membrane  Distillation  (MD)  process  utilizing  low-grade  heat energy. State-of-the-art review articles dealing with the integration of absolute low-grade heat sources with the MD process are limited to solar energy only. Available reviews in the literature on MD process, deal membranes chemistry [3], pore wetting/hydrophobicity [4], modelling [5,6]  or  MD  plant  installation  [7,8].  Few  reviews  discussing  the water-energy  nexus  focus  more  on  a  single  energy  source  such  as renewable  energy  sources  [9 -11].  Recent  reviews  on  MD  revealed designing of MD membranes [12 -15], fouling in MD [16,17], wastewater  recovery  [18],  oil  wastewater  treatment  [19].  In  addition,  no review article regarding MD process utilizing different energy sources for desalination is available. In this review, the suitability of the MD process  for  different  types  of  low-grade  heat  sources  (solar  energy, geothermal  energy  and  waste  heat  from  ships,  industries  and  power stations  and  secondary  heat  sources  such  as  those  derived  by  the transformation  of  primary  sources  like  crude  oil,  natural  gas,  wind, biomass hydroelectricity, etc.) is examined. A further novelty of this review includes a comparative economic analysis of MD systems powered by low-grade heat sources. The review is divided into the following five sections which are subsequently elaborated:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. Desalination
texts.text: 1.1. Desalination

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Desalination technologies are divided into two primary processes, viz. phase-change or thermal processes and membrane-based separation processes  [20 -23]  (Fig.  2).  Thermal  desalination  processes  involve vaporization, cooling and condensation of saline water, leaving behind highly  concentrated  water  (brine).  Among  thermal  desalination  processes, multistage flashing (MSF) is the most commonly used technology. However, desalination through MSF usually consumes 80.6 kWh heat per m 3 of freshwater produced [24]. Membrane-based desalination processes  depend  on  the  passage  of  water  through  a  semipermeable membrane  under  pressure.  Reverse  Osmosis  (RO)  is  a  widely  used technology among all membrane-based desalination processes, which requires ~3 kWh of power per m 3 of freshwater produced [24]. Despite its comparatively low cost, RO cannot be used for treating highly saline water,  and  disposal  of  concentrated  water  (reject)  poses  another problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD), a hybrid process (membrane/thermal), offers  several  unique  advantages  over  conventional  desalination  processes such as high water recovery rate, powered by low temperature and pressure, more flexibility, compactness and low maintenance [25, 26].  A  comparison  of  the  energy  consumption  and  cost  of  different desalination  technologies  are  presented  in  Table  1.  The  energy  consumption of the thermal-based processes (MSF, MED and MVC) is much higher than the membrane processes (RO, FO and ED). The energy requirements  for  the  thermal  desalination  technologies  are  practically independent of salt concentration, while the energy need for the membrane processes is exceedingly dependent on salt concentration [27].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2. Membrane Distillation
texts.text: 1.2. Membrane Distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD system was developed by Bodell in 1963 using silicon rubber membranes for saline water vapour diffusion [38], and Weyl patented an MD module with improved desalination efficiency in 1967 consisting of air-filled porous polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane [39]. In the same year, Findley published scientific articles on MD discussing its basic theory [40,41]. Findley ' s work was focused on improving the MD process in terms of energy efficiency and membrane cost. After a prolonged development process, improvement in MD technology gained new  interest  in  the  1980 s.  In  1985,  GORE,  an  American  company, invented the MD system for desalination utilizing a PTFE membrane [42]. The first two companies to market developed MD membranes were Swedish National Production Co. [43,44] and Enka AG [45,46]. All the collective efforts led to the idea that the highest energy efficiency could be achieved at high feed temperature. After a few years, two German companies  (Sick  and  SEP)  used  GORE  technologies  to  develop  a solar-powered desalination process. Few pilot MD  plants using low-grade heat were installed during the early 1980 s. Hogan et al. [47] developed a solar-powered MD (SPMD) unit of 50 LPD capacity using hollow fiber membranes. Internal heat recovery in AGMD modules was started early in the Memstill consortium by the University of Twente [48,49]. The economic analysis of the plant revealed negligible operating  costs  due  to  freely  available  energy.  This  study  significantly increased the worldwide application of low-grade heat-based MD processes in the 21st century. MD technology can be viewed as a cheaper alternative  to  conventional  water  treatment  processes  if  it  uses  a low-grade heat energy source.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3. Principle of MD
texts.text: 1.3. Principle of MD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD is a hybrid process since the single control unit regulates thermal evaporation and membrane separation processes. This process utilizes a hydrophobic  microporous  membrane,  which  selectively  permits  the vapour to move through the membrane while restricting the entry of feed water due to the hydrophobic nature of the membrane. This phenomenon leads to a pressure gradient due to the temperature deviation between the hot and cold water streams. The vapour in the permeate side gets condensed and is collected at the other side of the membrane [50 -55]. Thus, MD produces high-quality freshwater.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Application of low-grade heat in the MD process
texts.text: 2. Application of low-grade heat in the MD process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Depending  on  the  ease,  energy  may  be  transformed  into  certain types, and an energy source may be considered high or low-grade based on thermodynamic principles. Heat driven MD systems (modelling and experiments) can either use the direct form of heat or indirect form of heat, as shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b). The indirect form of arrangement is seldom  used  despite  its  better  heat  conservation  characteristic.  This indirect system (shown in Fig. 3(b)) provides the ability to collect and store heat at a much higher rate of flow in the heating circuit than the membrane circuit, meaning that heat can be collected and processed more rapidly [63].
texts.text: Depending  on  the  ease,  energy  may  be  transformed  into  certain types, and an energy source may be considered high or low-grade based on thermodynamic principles. Heat driven MD systems (modelling and experiments) can either use the direct form of heat or indirect form of heat, as shown in Fig. 4(a) and (b). The indirect form of arrangement is seldom  used  despite  its  better  heat  conservation  characteristic.  This indirect system (shown in Fig. 3(b)) provides the ability to collect and store heat at a much higher rate of flow in the heating circuit than the membrane circuit, meaning that heat can be collected and processed more rapidly [63].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gain output ratio (GOR) is a critical parameter for determining heat  recovery  efficiency  in  the  MD  process.  GOR,  a  dimensionless parameter during thermal desalination processes, is commonly used to quantify the distillation cycle ' s performance. It is defined as the latent heat of evaporation of the distillate water produced to the total heat input the cycle from the heat source. High saline water extracts heat and evaporates at relatively low temperatures, and water vapour penetrates through the membrane due to the slight water vapour pressure imbalance across the membrane. A regenerator reduces the heat absorbed from the heating source to minimize the module ' s  energy  input  and boost the system ' s energy efficiency.

texts.orig: 2.1. Solar energy
texts.text: 2.1. Solar energy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several MD units were designed and tested using solar energy devices like flat plate collector (FPC) [68,69], evacuated tube collector [70,71], compound parabolic collector (CPC) [72], solar ponds [73,74] and solar still [75,76]. Qtaishat and Banat [9] showed an overview of research on the coupling of MD with solar energy (low-grade heat). They summarized that the process is suitable for small capacities with high initial investment costs. Few pilot plants were also reported in the early 1980 s. Hogan et al. [47] developed a 50 LPD solar-powered domestic MD (SPMD) pilot plant using hollow fiber membranes. The economic analysis of the plant revealed 300 $ capital cost without any operational cost due to available energy resources. Banat ' s group has extensively worked on SPMD [9,68,69,75,77] and installed compact SPMD units in arid and semi-arid remote regions of Jordan [68,69]. The spiral-wound AGMD module with an internal heat recovery mechanism consisted of the desalination system. The FPC supplies the energy, and a PV panel supplies the auxiliary electrical electricity. Water is heated directly in the collector ' s absorber. Fig. 5 shows a schematic of the working of an SPMD using a solar thermal collector. Solar collectors can be used to provide  the  heat  (solar  thermal-solar  still,  solar  ponds)  or  electrical energy (solar photovoltaic) requirements to operate an MD system. A

The Memsys process is a hybrid desalination technology that combines the advantages of the efficient thermal multi-effect process such as energy efficiency, low operating cost, pressure and temperature with the VMD process into a small modular configuration [78]. The multi-effect design allows reusing energy as latent heat in the consecutive effects, and the latent heat of condensation in the condenser is recovered as sensible heat by preheating the feed through the condenser. Both actions increase heat efficiency [79].

texts.orig: The use of solar energy considerably reduces operating costs. However, its intermittent nature requires a non-stationary optimal operation that can be achieved by advanced control strategies such as practical nonlinear model predictive controller (PNMPC), model predictive control (MPC) [82] and mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) [83]. An unstable  solar  radiation  supply  from  sunrise  to  sunset  needs  to  be overcome using a technically and economically feasible system in solar heated  membrane  distillation  desalination  system  [84].  The  main drawback of using solar energy as a source is its unpredictable nature, which requires discontinuous operation and specific energy buffering systems means of advanced control [83].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Blanco  et  al.  [85]  assessed  the  techno-economic  feasibility  of multistage SPMD to establish a high-efficiency and cost-effective system for seawater desalination. The developed project showed an increase in the  capability  of  solar  desalination  based  on  MD.  Wang  et  al.  [74] established the possibility of extracting potable water from underwater using the hollow fiber VMD module combined with the solar energy collector. As the feed temperature increases, the results showed that less control is exerted on the permeate flux by the temperature polarization. Bouguecha et al. [86] show the experimental results of a solar-driven DCMD  plant  with  a  distillate  production  of  4.59  LPH  per  module. Although their system did not achieve expected thermal efficiency, the energy flow diagrams approved the positive impact of the heat recovery. A  solar-assisted  multistage  VMD  (SMVMD)  system  has  also  been developed [87]. A heat recovery unit was proposed to utilize energy recovery from permeate vapour to seawater supply. With the 24-stage solar-facilitated  VMD  system,  the  overall  water  generation  of  the SMVMD with ten heat recovery units is 3.37 m 3 /day, which is 34% more than the system with one heat recovery unit. The thermal energy consumption falls by 20%, increasing the amount of heat recovery units (HRUs) from 1 to 10 for a VMD without solar energy input. Ma et al. [88] recently designed and built a small VMD unit to provide drinking water in remote areas and provide electricity via direct solar heating through FPC PV panels. A modified thermal pumping technique was developed to overcome the heat-requiring feed recirculation and thermal condensing of the vapour to restore and condense latent heat without using a more intensive refrigeration system. Sensitivity and multi-objective  optimization  experiments  were  performed  based  on both pumping and cooling water output and electrical usage. Kumar and Martin  [89]  evaluated  optimum  design  criteria  for  developing  solar thermal  integrated  membrane  distillation  system  for  cogeneration  of pure water and heat. In this study, the application of cogeneration system for residential households in UAE is considered. Moore et al. [90] developed a comprehensive process model and designed an economically optimal system. Thermal energy for distillation was provided by solar thermal collectors and electricity was provided using photovoltaic collector. The cost of water in the optimized system was approximately $ 85 m 3 . Soomro et al. [91] investigated performance and cost comparison of different configurations of concentrated solar power plants (CSP) integrated with DCMD. Water production cost was found to be lowest (0.314 USD/m 3 ) for the solar powered plant integrated with the DCMD system. When adding anti-scalant to RO + MD, a base case cost of water (COW) of 0.633 USD m 3 and overall recovery of 84.59% was reached. The other setups had lower recovery, 66.93%, 72.73% and 73.38% for RO + CD + UF + MD,  RO + NF + MD and RO + NF + anti-scalant + MD,  respectively,  while  the  base  case  COW  of  these  setups  were 0.692, 0.7 and 0.698 USD m 3 , respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Geothermal energy
texts.text: 2.2. Geothermal energy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite the strong possibilities and advantages of geothermal energy in the field of MD-based desalination, only a limited number of studies are available. Sarbatly ' s group investigated the application of VMD for the production of freshwater using the energy from geothermal water [92,93]. They observed a significant reduction (59%) in water cost using geothermal energy for VMD compared to conventional energy sources. To avoid membrane pore wetting, they recommended the use of AGMD or  DCMD  rather  than  VMD.  National  Renewable  Energy  Laboratory (NREL), USA, initiated a project to evaluate the viability of the treatment of the blow-down of cooling towers with geothermal energy [94]. Fig. 6 shows a basic MD system integrated with a geothermal energy source [94]. Water vapour passes across the membrane from the hot-brine side to  the  cool-permeate  side.  Bouguecha  and  Dhahbi  [95]  integrated AGMD  with  a  fluidized  bed  crystallizer  (FBC)  to  remove  salt  from geothermal water. They expressed their concerns over the low recovery fraction despite favourable results using geothermal energy. To provide safe water in arsenic affected habitations in Latin America, low-enthalpy and waste geothermal energy sources combined with MD was used to produce freshwater [28,96]. Geothermal energy, including geothermal water  itself,  can  be  called  a  freshwater  supply  provided  via  the  MD method, a process best appropriate for use in areas where cheap heat sources are available [97].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Recovered waste heat
texts.text: 2.3. Recovered waste heat

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using heat from the exhaust, ships, natural gas compressor stations (NGCS),  and  power  stations,  MD  systems  have  produced  water  at  a feasible  cost  [98].  While  the  extremely  low  cost  of  waste  heat  is responsible for low process cost, membrane condenser is a promising technology for recovering water and waste heat. Generally, the membrane heat exchanger has been used with advantages (corrosion-proof, fouling  proof,  etc.)  over  other  conventional  heat  exchangers.  The attractive features of the membrane heat exchanger are high efficiency of heat recovery due to the heat and mass transfer across the membrane [99 -102].  A  membrane  heat  exchanger  can  be  used  for  heating  the seawater in the MD system using waste heat [103]. Schwantes et al. [104] performed experiments on large scale prototype structures using three  separate  plant  models  of  multiple  MD  modules.  They  demonstrated  that  waste  heat  from  combustion  engines  is  a  viable  energy source that offers the MD system an appropriate temperature.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.1. Waste heat from ships
texts.text: 2.3.1. Waste heat from ships

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermal desalination processes such as MED and MSF were used for freshwater production on ships. Currently, there is a shift in this trend towards the MD process, as evident from scientific reports in which the input heat to MD is derived from the ship ' s cooling water [105 -108]. Xu et al. [105] reported a pilot VMD system using waste heat from ships for examining operational conditions of polypropylene (PPE) hollow fibers. The  feed  water  was  heated  by  heat  generated  by  vessel  motor,  and 99.99% salt rejection with 5.4 LMH flux was obtained. Comparative studies of various MD structures and membranes to allow possible use of waste heat produced in the engine cooling system have carried out onboard cruise vessels [106]. In any test, the permeate content was good, and virtually all ions were extracted. Later, Koo et al. [107] tried to improve  the  efficiency  of  the  VMD  device  in  collaboration  with  the waste heat produced by the ship utilizing experiments under various operating conditions. They demonstrated that the membrane modules had  a  rejection  of > 99% for optimized  operating  conditions.  Fig.  7 shows  the  MD  system  used  in  combination  with  the  ship  engine  by utilizing the waste heat used in their study. Very recently, [108] used the heat from the ship ' s engine cooling water to multistage AGMD. They could generate 1 -15 m 3 per day of freshwater by using waste heat only provided the membrane surface area of 0.25 m 2 .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3.2. Waste heat from industries and power stations
texts.text: 2.3.2. Waste heat from industries and power stations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Waste heat is available in abundance near industries, power stations, etc. Such type of heat can be utilized with MD for the production of freshwater  in  remote  area  etc.  The  viability  of  industrial  low-grade waste  heat  for  desalination  by  MD  was  studied  by  Khraisheh  et  al. [109]. Fig. 8 shows a schematic of the MD plant powered by waste heat from a thermal power plant. Dow et al. [110] used waste heat generated from the gas-fired power plant for wastewater treatment using DCMD. This was performed without removing the membranes or the assembly for three months. The water recovery was reported to be 92.8% with flux 3 LMH at 40 ◦ C. Ali et al. [111] experimentally investigated the performance of MD using low grade of waste heat available at flue gas desulphurization. They found that the membrane characterized by high porosity can offer higher flux and lower specific energy utilization. They observed improvement in the membrane flux by 15% on increasing the feed temperature from 30 ◦ C to 45 ◦ C. Later, Okare et al. [112] used DCMD  process  water  treatment  produced  during  the  production  of natural gas from shale reservoirs. They integrated DCMD with waste heat from natural gas compressor stations. The energy analysis showed that waste heat from compressor stations in natural gas is adequate to concentrate  all  the  water  produced  to  30 wt%,  despite  the  initial salinity.  To  generate  freshwater,  electricity  from  portable  electrical power generators, Morciano et al. [113] developed small-scale desalination equipment (temperature below 80 ◦ C). They found low heat to be the  best  suitable  for  MD  systems  with  a  lightweight  distiller,  which provides  the  attractive  feature  of  low  temperatures  operations.  Very recently,  Silva  et  al.  [114]  argued  that  the  water  footprint  could  be reduced  by  using  heat  recovery  MD  systems.  The  system  produces freshwater without any high cost of energy.
texts.text: Waste heat is available in abundance near industries, power stations, etc. Such type of heat can be utilized with MD for the production of freshwater  in  remote  area  etc.  The  viability  of  industrial  low-grade waste  heat  for  desalination  by  MD  was  studied  by  Khraisheh  et  al. [109]. Fig. 8 shows a schematic of the MD plant powered by waste heat from a thermal power plant. Dow et al. [110] used waste heat generated from the gas-fired power plant for wastewater treatment using DCMD. This was performed without removing the membranes or the assembly for three months. The water recovery was reported to be 92.8% with flux 3 LMH at 40 ◦ C. Ali et al. [111] experimentally investigated the performance of MD using low grade of waste heat available at flue gas desulphurization. They found that the membrane characterized by high porosity can offer higher flux and lower specific energy utilization. They observed improvement in the membrane flux by 15% on increasing the feed temperature from 30 ◦ C to 45 ◦ C. Later, Okare et al. [112] used DCMD  process  water  treatment  produced  during  the  production  of natural gas from shale reservoirs. They integrated DCMD with waste heat from natural gas compressor stations. The energy analysis showed that waste heat from compressor stations in natural gas is adequate to concentrate  all  the  water  produced  to  30 wt%,  despite  the  initial salinity.  To  generate  freshwater,  electricity  from  portable  electrical power generators, Morciano et al. [113] developed small-scale desalination equipment (temperature below 80 ◦ C). They found low heat to be the  best  suitable  for  MD  systems  with  a  lightweight  distiller,  which provides  the  attractive  feature  of  low  temperatures  operations.  Very recently,  Silva  et  al.  [114]  argued  that  the  water  footprint  could  be reduced  by  using  heat  recovery  MD  systems.  The  system  produces freshwater without any high cost of energy.

texts.orig: Wang  and  Chung  [121]  developed  a  hybrid  desalination  process using freeze desalination (FD) and MD to utilize the waste heat energy released  from  the  regasification  of  liquefied  natural  gas  (LNG).  The technology  model  was  exhibited  by  indirect-contact  FD  (ICFD)  and DCMD  configurations. By optimizing the operation parameters, high-quality  drinkable water (salinity:  0.144 g L 1 )  was  produced by ICFD,  while  ultra-pure  water  (salinity:  0.062 g L 1 )  was  obtained  by DCMD.  About  71.5%  of  water  was  recovered,  which  proved  the energy-saving  nature  of  the  hybrid  processes.  Khayet  et  al.  [122] explored the probability of developing a hybrid DCMD system with a nuclear power reactor. Their experimental findings verified the feasibility of water desalination by the absorption of nuclear-generated heat and electricity.

The closing-loop mechanism puts together MD pure water flows employing thermal isolation and RO, converting energy from electricity using a hydro-turbine. They reported a hybrid RO-MD system with 81.6% energy efficiency of Carnot. In the mechanical vapour compression (MVC) method,  Swaminathan  et  al.  [124]  replaced  the preheater heat exchanger with an MD setup to extract thermal energy from the salt water stream. MD as a heat exchanger ensures that MD has the thermal capacity and a reduction in maintenance cost as the MD module substitutes inefficient heat exchanger devices. They found a 6% reduction in water  costs  generated  from  the  hybrid  MVC-MD  system  than  the standalone MVC system. [125] demonstrated the synergetic integration of the VMD and adsorption desalination (AD) where the useful effect of AD  cycle  is  channelled  to  boost  the  operation  of  the  VMD  process, namely  the  low  vacuum  environment  to  maintain  the  high  pressure gradient across the microporous hydrophobic membrane. texts.orig: novel thermal cogeneration system featuring potable water with MD has been designed and tested in combination with domestic hot water supply [128]. [129] developed the MD device using thermal energy and generated high condensed salty streams, while reverses electrodialysis (RED) transformed Gibbe ' s free mixing power from the salty streams produced into electricity. These findings demonstrated that the optimal electrical output in the proposed MD-RED method occurs at specified operating temperatures in different feeding concentration ranges at an optimum relative permeate/feed solution flow rate. For 5 mol/kg NaCl feed concentration in MD, about 1.15% electrical efficiency of hybrid MD-RED  system  was  obtained  in  the  range  of  20 -60 ◦ C,  which confirmed the potential application of low-grade heat harvesting. Subsequently, Micari et al. [130] coupled RED with MD and evaluated the executions  of  the  integrated  system  under  different  operating  conditions.  Based  on  the  optimized  design  parameters  obtained.  Lai  et  al. [131] recovered waste heat from proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC)  for  the  desalination  of  saline  water  by  the  PEMFC-DCMD hybrid process. An acceptable PEMFC current density and DCMD mass flow  rate  confirmed  optimum  fuel  energy  output.  The  energy  consumption  decreased  by  201 266% in  comparison  with  the  PEMFC system. Later, Tan et al. [132] improved energy efficiency by hybridizing the heat pump with an MD unit for simultaneous space cooling and water treatment. Efficiency per unit energy doubled for the hybrid device  in  contrast  with  the  thermoelectric  dehumidifier.  The  cold  air recycling influenced the condensate flow without a dramatic cooling loss relative to other parameters measured during the process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The thermal energy is the most critical parameter for the cost integrated with the MD process. Besides, electrical power is required to drive the pump to pump the feed water and permeate [54]. A few works of literature have cited the cost of the MD process and its effectiveness when used with different energy sources like electricity, waste  heat, solar energy, geothermal energy, etc. [34,85,98,138,139]. Since the MD system can be handled effectively with a low grade of heat energy than the other conventional distillation process, the MD system can produce water at a meagre cost when waste heat is available, which can be used for the industrial application [138]. The membrane material properties, type of membrane system also economize the MD system, and the water production cost increases at lower porosity when the thickness of the membrane module increases. The AGMD provides the water at a lower cost than DCMD at lower porosity [140]. Soomro et al. [141] discussed the performance and economic assessment of the integrated parabolic-trough plant with DCMD for simultaneous energy and freshwater production. It was estimated that the maximum and minimum electric energy generation would be 13.5 GWh or 7.71 GWh. The economic estimate revealed that the average and actual power rate was 24.54 cents per kWh and 19.3833 cents per kWh, respectively. The MD process  is  more  economical  than  other  desalination  processes  if  the waste heat used as the source of energy in the MD process [142]. The lifetime  of  the  membrane  is  key  factors  when  evaluating  the  cost  of water  production.  When  the  membrane  and/or  plant  life  grows,  the costs decline [77].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water production cost (WPC) model that researchers generally use includes a capital cost (direct + indirect) and operation and maintenance costs. The direct capital cost includes membrane and module cost, installation cost, and land cost for the system, whereas indirect capital  cost  includes  the  cost  associated  with  the  legal  fee,  labour charges,  contingencies,  administrative  fee,  and  insurance  fee.  The operation and maintenance cost includes membrane replacement cost, brine  disposal  cost,  electricity  charges,  and  cost  attributed  to  the maintenance  of  the  system.  An  accurate  model  must  include  all  the above factors when estimating the cost of the system.

[144],  and  it  was observed that cost reduced significantly when water heating was achieved by natural gas compressor station integrated with MD system. The cost of water produced from the MD system was estimated to be about $0.74 per m 3 and $5.70 per m 3 with and without the integration of waste heat,  respectively.  Lee  et  al.  [145]  suggested  using  multistage-VMD instead of VMD because specific energy consumption (SEC) increased multistage productivity. The cost of water production was significantly reduced with the utilization of waste heat. With recovered heat, the cost per m 3 water was reduced to $ 0.52 from $ 1.16 when the waste heat was used. The SPMD system produced water at a higher rate than the RO unit, and about 70% of the total cost associated with the SPMD system was due to solar collectors. Further, the production cost for SPMD was higher than the PV-RO hybrid system [9]. The DCMD process has the highest potential for the treatment of highly saline water, where the RO cannot be a feasible solution [112]. The economic analysis of the DCMD system confirmed about $ 1.17 per m 3 water production cost considering heat recovery [146]. A comparative study for RO and MD driven by low-grade heat energy revealed $ 2.2 and $ 0.66 per m 3 desalination cost, respectively [147]. Hence, MD driven by low-grade heat energy and/or heat recovery process is economical compared to RO. In another

texts.orig: widespread  application  of  the  MD  process  for  desalination  of  highsalinity water using a low-grade heat source. The major challenges of the MD process are concentration polarization, high energy consumption  and  cost,  temperature  polarization,  and  scaling/fouling  on  the surface of the membrane due to the contaminants present in the feed solution [119,155 -157]. Pore wetting and fouling are two MD interrelated phenomena that interact and present major problems [156,157]. They are associated with all the desalination processes, and MD is no exception  in  this  regard.  Several  researchers  have  reported  severe fouling in MD processes [158,159]. These can impede the widespread application of the MD process during high-salinity water treatment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD appears  to  be  a  very  promising  process,  particularly  in  lowtemperature  solar,  waste,  or  other  heat.  However,  it  is  clear  that  a more  intensive  research  and  development  effort,  both  in  terms  of experimentation and modelling, is required, focusing on key issues such as long-term liquid/vapour selectivity, membrane ageing and fouling, feed  water  contamination,  and  heat  recovery  optimization.  Scale-up studies and realistic assessments of basic operating parameters on real pilot  plants,  including  cost  and  long-term  stability,  are  also  deemed necessary [160]. From a practical viewpoint, the varied complexity of the low-grade heat sources such as variable temperature, supply, flow, and availability influences the application significantly. The continuous availability  of  low-grade  heat  source  is  another  challenge  for  MD working on low-grade heat. Hence for practical applications, scalability appears to be an issue. An economic analysis for this platform has not yet been  developed.  As  a  result,  an  economic  model  that  includes  cost estimation  could  be  created.  Additional  research  on  solar/fossil  fuel hybrid  membrane  distillation  systems  is  required,  particularly  waste heat from decentralized thermal power systems for water and power cogeneration,  as  both  are  critical  in  remote  areas.  Waste  heat  from decentralized systems could reduce fuel consumption and overcome the intermittent nature of the solar source. In terms of energy, a desalination plant powered by renewable energy is likely to be a standalone system in an area without an electricity grid [161]. Low-temperature solar thermal collection systems, which use relatively low-cost and straightforward  components,  could  potentially  be  used  for  MD.  Solar  energy ' s scalability,  lack  of  emissions,  and  geographic  availability  make  it  a promising potential source of heat energy for MD. However, practical implementation of solar systems will necessitate the removal of several impediments. Solar collectors with a large enough physical footprint to provide enough energy for reasonable water production may be prohibitively expensive [162]. In addition, only a small percentage of the feed water can be recovered in a single pass [163]. From a market and corporate context, capital expense and payback time are the main considerations regarded by the management boards of manufacturing enterprises.  The  consequences  of  these  issues  must  be  minimized  to achieve the optimum results of the MD process. The energy requirement for  plant  operation  significantly  increases  the  operation  and  maintenance cost, and the use of low-grade heat will reduce the product water cost with a considerable margin. Given the importance of capital and operational costs that are not directly related to energy consumption, such as maintenance, the maximization of energy efficiency of an MD desalination system is not always analogous to minimizing water costs. But, the incorporation of heat at no cost surely leads to a reduction in cost through a huge margin.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Opportunities and recent installations
texts.text: 4.2. Opportunities and recent installations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MD  is  an  attractive  water  desalination  technology,  but  the  low transmembrane water flux and the inherent low energy efficiency are significant drawbacks compared to other desalination technologies. A successful combination of process engineering and materials research helps to cope with these constraints. For commercializing and popularizing the process, membrane and system design improvements are necessary  and  provide  an  opportunity  to  improve  the  MD  process further. The selection of an efficient MD membrane (suitable membrane forming material, wettability, and other desired properties) is a crucial step,  significantly  affecting  the  distillation  process ' s  MD  system  efficiency and productivity [164]. During the last few decades, modifications  in  MD  plant  layout  and  operating  conditions  to  improve  the performance have been carried out [140,165 -167]. Membrane ' s resistance and thermal transfer can dramatically affect the energy efficiency of MD. The overall efficiency of the MD process can be increased by increasing membrane thickness, hydrophobicity, feed water temperature, and decreasing tortuosity [26,64]. However, suitable membrane optimization is also required to control wettability, dimensional, and thermal instability. Generally, 0.3 µ m pore diameter for efficient MD membranes is recommended to balance the liquid entry pressure and flux  [166].  The  temperature  and  concentration  polarization  on  the membrane surface can be reduced by increasing the feed circulation rate.  MD  is  advantageous  when  the  operation  is  carried  out  at  low pressure as it increases the vapour permeability. To improve thermal efficiency and reduce cost, module configuration to enhance heat recovery such as conductive gap and multi channel module have been proposed or new process configuration [168,169].

texts.orig: provide commercial MD plants which use low-grade energy as a heat source  [8,170].  A  number  of  MD  pilot  plants  have  been  installed  in several countries which use low-grade heat energy. The Aquiva foundation collaborated  with Maldives  State  Electric.  Co.  Ltd. to  provide drinking water on the country ' s inhabited islands using waste heat from generators through MD [171]. In the MEDINA project, a pilot MD plant was installed in a village of orphans in the coastal region Mahres, which uses  only  solar  energy  as  an  energy  source.  The  plant  produces 35 -70 l h 1 freshwater per hour with 4 m 2 hollow fiber membrane area [172]. Banat ' s group installed compact SPMD units in arid and semi-arid remote regions of Jordan [68,69]. Scarab development AB installed two MD pilot  plants  in  Sweden  using  a  thermal  cogeneration  plant  with 1 -2 m 3 per day production of freshwater [173]. The plants were also used for the treatment of municipal wastewater. The plants used the district heating network as a thermal energy source. The Global MVP project initiated by North Korea focused on the scaling-up of MD; PRO was able to simultaneously recover water and energy from SWRO brine with high efficiency [174]. Aquastill MD systems installed in Almería (Spain) with membrane area 25.9 m 2 produce 75.11 l h 1 freshwater using solar energy [8,175]. Memstill consortium installed MD systems in Jurong Island (Singapore) to produce freshwater using waste heat from petroleum refinery [8]. Chembrane Research & Engineering Inc .,  China, developed an AGMD module with internal heat recovery in a parallel arrangement which produced 9.2 l m 2  h 1 distillate product with high efficiency and selectivity [176].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The application of desalination technologies is growing in developed countries, and efforts are being made to significantly reduce the membrane and capital cost along with energy consumption. MD is a promising  desalination  technology  that  can  help  reduce  worldwide  water energy stress sustainably. MD has demonstrated tremendous potential in seawater/hypersaline  water  desalination  when  combined  with  traditional seawater desalination systems such as MSF, ED, RO MED, and FO. The  intrinsic  modular  and  scalability  properties  of  the  MD  process makes it beneficial. Continuous development of membrane material and module engineering in MD technology is evident from the literature. Nevertheless, the feasibility of MD as an energy-efficient desalination cycle remains elusive, even when advances in membrane technology and process configurations are under process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using  low-grade  heat  energy,  MD  can  improve  the  water  supply situation and play a vital role in the water-energy nexus. This review discussed  the  opportunities  for  hybrid  MD  systems  (FO-MD,  ED-MD, MVC-MD,  RED-MD,  PEMFC-MD)  to  increase  water  production  and overall energy efficiency with zero liquid discharge strategy. Current literature suggests that the inexpensive energy sources discussed in this review, like solar energy, geothermal energy, and waste heat recovered from ships, industries, and power stations, can make the MD process more sustainable and cheaper. The membrane properties and design of the  process  also  have  a  major  effect  on  the  energy  efficiency  of  the process. The techno-economic analysis indicates that the MD process can be  economical  compared  to  conventional  desalination  techniques because of the utilization of energy from low-grade heat sources. The MD desalination capacity is expected to inflate in the coming times, reducing energy consumption and water production costs.
